column1: The New Frontier of Exotic DNA
column3: Understanding the science of the Cal Gene (SLC45A2) and the OKC health protocol for Pink French Bulldogs.
column4:
The New Frontier of Exotic DNA

In the world of elite French Bulldog breeding, the "Pink" Frenchie has become the gold standard of rarity. But at OKC Frenchies, we know that true value isn’t just about a visual—it’s about the science behind the pigment. To understand a Pink Frenchie, you have to understand the Cal Gene.

What is the Cal Gene?

The "Pink" look is a specific form of oculocutaneous albinism, caused by a mutation in the SLC45A2 gene (the caL locus). Unlike a "Cream" dog that retains black or liver pigment on the nose and pads, a true Pink Frenchie lacks eumelanin entirely. This results in:
  • The Glow: Translucent, pinkish skin and nose.
  • The Eyes: Bright, light-colored eyes.
  • The Signature: A soft coat sheen that defines the OKC line.

The OKC Health Protocol: Foundation Over Everything

A common misconception is that "Pink" equals "Poor Health." We prove that wrong daily. Our Pink pups aren't just vibrant; they are structurally and internally superior because of the Raw & Paw Protocol. We don't use fillers or synthetic fixes. Because our dogs start with a Raw Base, they have the natural enzymes and nutrients required for a biological baseline. From there, we stack for excellence:
  1. Goat Brew: Our primary delivery system for essential probiotics and cellular hydration.
  2. Plasma: For a daily immunity and recovery boost to support exotic DNA.
  3. Omega Mania: A multi-oil anchor that reinforces the skin barrier and coat health.

Investing in Genetic Capital

For the serious breeder, a Pink Frenchie is Genetic Capital. Integrating the Cal gene into a program requires a deep understanding of recessive traits and a commitment to elite husbandry. When you purchase an OKC Pink, you aren't just buying a dog—you are buying a decade of breeding expertise and a proven nutritional roadmap for success.

column1: The Ancient Red Domino: Mastering the eA Gradient
column3: Explaining the eA Ancient Red (Domino) gene in French Bulldogs: How it creates the signature two-tone "Husky" fur strand, inheritance patterns with cream (e), and the 50/50 visual odds for elite breeding programs.
column4:
The Science of the Visual Shift

While standard fawns and brindles are classic, the eA (Ancient Red/Domino) gene is the new frontier for breeders chasing that high-definition, multi-dimensional look. Originally found in Northern breeds, eA creates a striking "visual shift" in Frenchies that mimics a textured, double-coat appearance.

The "Two-Tone" Fur Science

What makes eA so unique is how it interacts with individual strands of fur. Instead of a solid color, eA creates a gradient effect:
  • The Root: Often lighter, tan or cream-based.
  • The Tip: Retains the darker pigment (Ancient Red or Domino shading).
  • The Result: Each strand looks two-toned, giving the dog a vibrant "glow" that standard DNA simply can't produce.

Breeding Math: The 50/50 Rule

Understanding the hierarchy of the E-Locus is critical for your production roadmap. The eA gene is dominant to "little e" (cream), but recessive to the wild type (E) and Mask (Em). When you breed a Visual eA (eA/e) to a Genetic Cream (e/e):
  1. 50% Visual eA: These pups will carry the Domino pattern (eA/e).
  2. 50% Creams: These pups will be standard recessive red/cream (e/e).

The "Ghost" eA: Masking

The eA gene is a master of disguise. It can be almost entirely masked by a Melanistic Mask (Em).
  • Em/eA: The black mask may appear "smeared," but the eA stays hidden under the mask’s dominance.
  • E/eA: You might see a slight "widow’s peak" marking on the head, but the dog won't show the full "Husky" mask.

Protecting the DNA Capital

Because eA restricts dark pigment, these dogs require high-level nutrition to maintain that luster. To keep that "Ancient Red" vibrant, we recommend our Raw & Paw stack—specifically Plasma for cellular immunity and Omega Mania to feed the hair follicle from the inside out.

column1: The Genetics of Texture: Floodles, Furnishings, and the RSPO2 Truth
column3: Debunking the "Poodle blood" myth: Why the Floodle or Teddy Bear look is actually a calculated genetic combination of FGF5 and RSPO2, and how elite husbandry defines coat quality over lineage.
column4:
The Genetics of Texture: It’s Not About the Breed

In the exotic Frenchie world, there is a massive debate: Poodle crosses vs. Wire-hair crosses. Some breeders swear that "Poodle blood" creates a better coat. At OKC Frenchies, we know the truth: The gene doesn't remember the breed. The "Floodle" or "Teddy Bear" look isn't a magical breed secret—it is a specific genetic combination of two distinct markers: FGF5 (Fluffy) and RSPO2 (Furnishings).

The Furnishing Gene (RSPO2)

Furnishings is a dominant gene. Even on a short-haired dog, one copy of RSPO2 will display a longer, wiry texture around the face and legs. Whether that gene originated from a Poodle or a Wire-hair is irrelevant to the DNA; once it is in the Frenchie line, it functions as a standalone trait.

The "Floodle" Threshold: Two Copies of Fluffy

While one copy of RSPO2 creates the "Wire-hair" look, the true Teddy Bear or Floodle aesthetic occurs when the furnishing gene is paired with two copies of the FGF5 (Fluffy) gene.
  • Fluffy (L/L or FGF5): Controls the length of the coat.
  • Furnishings (RSPO2): Controls the "facial furniture" and the texture/curl.
The result is a coat that can display as straight, wavy, or curly. Every dog displays these genes differently based on their individual DNA makeup—not because of which ancestor brought the gene into the line generations ago.

Coat Quality: It’s Husbandry, Not Heritage

The quality of a Floodle’s coat isn't determined by its lineage—it’s determined by its Protocol. We have seen "Poodle-heavy" lines with brittle, dry coats and "Wire-hair" lines with silk. The secret to a world-class Teddy Bear coat is the OKC Core Protocol:
  1. Omega Mania: Essential for hydrating the longer fibers of an FGF5/RSPO2 coat to prevent breakage.
  2. Goat Brew: Maintains the gut health that prevents the skin inflammation often seen in long-coated exotics.
  3. Plasma: Supports the high metabolic demand of growing and maintaining an elite "Floodle" coat.

Buying the Science, Not the Hype

When you invest in an OKC Floodle, you aren't buying a "cross-breed" marketing story. You are buying a genetically mapped, health-tested, and nutritionally supported elite French Bulldog.
